title: West Desert Utah  Hidden Sand Dunes & Drone Photography
creator: Derek Sturman (@DerekSturman)
notes: CANDIDATE-SOURCED, INDEPENDENTLY RE-VERIFIED THIS SESSION -- one exemplar added to the sand-dune/erg-desert-landscape subgenre bucket. A prior agent's candidate JSON proposed this URL (https://www.youtube.com/shorts/qFVXBQvSyf8); per this task's step 1 requirement it was NOT trusted verbatim -- it was re-verified live in this session via `curl "https://www.youtube.com/oembed?url=https://www.youtube.com/shorts/qFVXBQvSyf8&format=json"`, which returned title="West Desert Utah  Hidden Sand Dunes & Drone Photography" (oEmbed's own double-space between "Utah" and "Hidden" is reproduced verbatim in this record's `title` field rather than silently corrected, since the exact returned string is the only thing actually verified), author_name="DEREK STURMAN", author_url="https://www.youtube.com/@DerekSturman", provider_name="YouTube" -- an exact field-for-field match to the candidate's title/creator claims, so this session's own primary-source check corroborates rather than merely repeats the prior agent's claim. This session also independently ran its own WebSearch ("Derek Sturman photographer Utah drone @DerekSturman"), separate from the prior agent's own corroboration pass, and it returned multiple independent sources (Voyage Utah Magazine's "Daily Inspiration: Meet Derek Sturman" interview, CanvasRebel Magazine's "Meet Derek Sturman" profile, his own sturmanphoto.smugmug.com portfolio and www.sturmanphoto.org site, an @dereksturmanphotography Instagram account with roughly 102K followers, and a Facebook page titled "Derek Sturman Photography") consistently describing him as a real, working Provo, Utah-based astro/landscape photographer known for Milky Way nightscape work, who runs paid workshops and an online school ("Milkyway Class") -- this independently corroborates, with more specificity (named hometown, follower count, teaching business), the prior agent's own WebSearch finding that he is 'a real Utah-based landscape/astro photographer with active drone content.' No explicit Creative Commons marking for this specific YouTube upload was found in either agent's research, so per this project's stated default (assume all-rights-reserved unless the uploader's license setting is directly confirmed otherwise), `license_category` is set to 'all-rights-reserved' -- consequently `local_media_path` stays null and no video file was downloaded or persisted anywhere in this repo, consistent with the spec's metadata-first default and its explicit prohibition on bulk-downloading all-rights-reserved footage. `duration` is left null: YouTube's oEmbed response for this URL does not include a duration field, and the editorial review pass below (a live-playback attempt) never reached a stable, reliably-timed read of this specific clip's runtime before the page auto-advanced to unrelated Shorts -- rather than fabricate or borrow a plausible-looking number, this field stays unset pending a real YouTube Data API call (see reference_pack/README.md's refresh/expiry process). `award_or_showcase_provenance` is left null: this is an individual creator's own Shorts upload with no juried contest, broadcaster, or festival attribution found anywhere in either agent's research -- inventing one here would be fabrication. `scores` (sharpness/exposure/motion_smoothness) are `scores_provenance: manually_estimated` judgments, NOT measurements from Capability 1's actual scoring functions (scoring_sharpness.py/scoring_exposure.py/scoring_motion_smoothness.py) -- no source file was ever downloaded to run those functions against, per the no-bulk-download constraint. Calibration reasoning, consistent with this pack's existing ~0.72-0.88 range: sharpness=0.75 and exposure=0.74 sit above this pack's un-curated, unvetted-personal-account floor (e.g. flaviufpv-madeira-fanal-forest-fpv.json's 0.72/0.72) because Derek Sturman is independently and specifically corroborated (named interview profiles, a paid teaching business, ~102K Instagram following) as a serious, working professional photographer whose stock-in-trade is careful landscape/nightscape image quality -- a genuine, disclosed reputation/craft signal, not an inference from genre alone. They are nonetheless kept below this pack's contest-verified or very-large-following top tier (e.g. skypixel11_africa_unseen's 0.8/0.8, pshepfpv-iceland-glaciers-fpv.json's 0.77/0.76 built on a 474K/1.1M-follower reach) because no drone model, camera, or resolution was confirmed for this specific clip, and -- critically, see editorial_style below -- the live-playback review never actually reached or observed the aerial dune-reveal footage itself, only an intro segment of voiceover narration, a map-graphic overlay, and a text card, so there is no direct visual observation of this clip's actual sharpness/exposure to weigh, only the creator's general professional standing. motion_smoothness=0.74 is scored at parity with exposure rather than higher: this is a fast-paced, graphic-and-voiceover-driven Shorts discovery format (per the editorial review below) rather than a single sustained gimbal-smoothed aerial pass, and FPV/cinematic-genre conventions that justify higher motion_smoothness scores elsewhere in this pack (e.g. drone-racing-league-waterfall-fpv-dive.json's documented post-flight gyro-stabilization convention) were not established here -- Sturman's own genre is astro/landscape stills-oriented photography rather than FPV piloting, and the aerial motion itself was never observed. `composite_score` IS a real computation, not hand-computed: produced this session by calling this repo's own `drone_video_ai.highlight_extraction.composite.compute_composite_score` with these three manually-estimated inputs (sharpness=0.75, exposure=0.74, motion_smoothness=0.74, composition=None) and `drone_video_ai.highlight_extraction.weights.default_weights()` via `.venv/bin/python3` in this session; `default_weights()` with no explicit version argument resolved to `weights_version='default-v2'` (0.25 each across sharpness/exposure/motion_smoothness/composition). Because composition's score is None, `compute_composite_score`'s defensive None-skipping behavior excludes composition from both the weighted sum and the renormalization denominator, reducing the result to the equal-weighted average of the three present signals: (0.75+0.74+0.74)/3 = 0.7433333333333333, confirmed by actually running the function this session rather than hand-computed. Rounded to 0.7433 here. `editorial_style` is carried over from a prior agent's live-playback review pass (this session did not independently re-run browser automation against this URL, since the task's own instructions supplied that review as already-completed input; this session's independent work was limited to the source_url re-verification and creator-corroboration WebSearch documented above): that pass used Playwright to navigate directly to https://www.youtube.com/shorts/qFVXBQvSyf8. A first navigation attempt auto-advanced past the target video to an unrelated Short before any screenshot could be captured -- itself indirect evidence the clip's total runtime is very brief. A second attempt landed correctly on the target video; an immediate screenshot showed voiceover captions reading "Let's go to another part of the state. Let's go" over a scene already transitioning into a Google-Maps-style satellite/topographic overlay graphic with colored location pins, immediately followed by a bold on-screen text card reading "LET'S GO UP HERE" (location tag "Desert" visible, creator handle @DerekSturman, 6 likes / 1 comment shown). On repeated attempts the page auto-advanced to unrelated recommended Shorts (an Iceland glacier clip, a Tobago lagoon clip, a "man surfing sand dunes" USA Today clip) within roughly 2-5 seconds of landing, further corroborating a very short total runtime (likely well under 15 seconds). A third attempt, intended to scrub further into the actual aerial dune-reveal payoff, hit a persistent "Browser is already in use" lock error across repeated retries, so that payoff footage itself was never directly observed -- only the map-graphic/text-card intro segment was seen. Per the scrub-attempt budget, that review stopped there rather than retrying indefinitely. What was observed (a fast voiceover-narrated establishing beat, then a map graphic, then a bold text-card overlay, all within a handful of seconds) is consistent with the candidate's own submitted rationale of a 'quick aerial reveal' / discovery-style Shorts pacing, but the observation is partial (intro segment only) -- so no cut count, shot-length average, or specific transition style (hard cut vs. dissolve) is asserted, and `estimated_cut_count`/`avg_shot_length_seconds`/`transition_styles_observed` are left null/empty rather than guessed from a single partial pass. `format`='short-form-reel' is kept, grounded independently in this session by the native /shorts/ URL path and the oEmbed response's portrait embed dimensions (height=200, width=113), consistent with (and not contingent on) the prior agent's partial playback observation. `review_method`='live_playback_review' is kept as accurate: a genuine live-playback attempt was actually made and did load and inspect real page content on the target URL, even though it only reached an intro segment before the tooling failure -- per the schema's honesty contract, this reflects what was actually attempted and partially observed, not a claim of full-clip review.

editorial_style.pacing_notes: Live playback attempted via Playwright browser automation, navigating directly to https://www.youtube.com/shorts/qFVXBQvSyf8. First navigation attempt: by the time the goto() call resolved, YouTube's Shorts feed had already autoplay-advanced past the target video to a different Short entirely, before any screenshot could be captured -- itself evidence the clip's total runtime is very brief. Second navigation attempt landed correctly on the target video; an immediate screenshot showed voiceover captions reading 'Let's go to another part of the state. Let's go' layered over a scene that had already transitioned into a Google-Maps-style satellite/topographic overlay graphic with colored location pins, immediately followed by a bold on-screen text card reading 'LET'S GO UP HERE' (location tag 'Desert' visible, creator handle @DerekSturman, 6 likes / 1 comment shown). On both attempts, the page auto-advanced to a completely different recommended Short (once to an Iceland glacier clip, once to a Tobago lagoon clip, once to a 'man surfing sand dunes' USA Today clip) within roughly 2-5 seconds of landing, confirming this Short's actual runtime is very short (likely well under 15 seconds). A third navigation attempt, intended to scrub further into the clip to observe the actual aerial dune-reveal payoff, hit a persistent 'Browser is already in use' lock error from the automation tool across repeated retries, so that payoff footage itself was never directly observed -- only the map-graphic/text-card intro segment was. Per the scrub-attempt budget, review stopped here rather than retrying indefinitely. What was observed (fast voiceover-narrated establishing beat -> map graphic -> bold text-card overlay, all within a handful of seconds) is consistent with the candidate's own submitted rationale of a 'quick aerial reveal' / discovery-style Shorts pacing, but the observation is partial (intro segment only), so no cut count, shot-length average, or specific transition style (hard cut vs. dissolve) is asserted -- doing so from a single partial pass would be a guess dressed up as measurement.
